our_argument_summary: Our review states that the 2.26× longitudinal-to-latitudinal NMP drift ratio is mathematically extracted from publicly available NOAA NP.xy pole-position data, has no derivation from dome geometry (the dome's H(r) field equation is axially symmetric and cannot generate longitude-dependent quantities), and is fully explained by standard core-mantle boundary flux-lobe dynamics (Livermore et al. 2020). The new C3 detail_verdict_text adds a separate post-hoc-failure argument: per the dome author's own definition, no monitor.py entry exists for this value, and NOAA NP.xy is an indexed public dataset that fails the legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt — WIN-043 listed CONFIRMED with no derivation shown
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W019 'NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral' marked PENDING in dome's snapshot

data/predictions.json — W019 entry: our_verdict='falsified' (verdict_history: changed standard_physics→falsified 2026-04-14, restored 2026-04-20 after silent revert); dome's author_status='refined' with notes that lateral drift is 5.5× larger than poleward
data/predictions.json — PRED-R004 'NMP drift ratio ≥2.0× through 2028' our_verdict='recycled' (forward-looking restatement of WIN-043)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py is not in the current raw-text snapshot; per the C1/C2 chain, Domain #11 fetches NOAA NP.xy and only checks whether current longitude > 130°E. No domain decomposes drift into latitudinal/longitudinal velocity components or computes the 2.26× ratio. The ratio appears only as homepage text and is unmonitored by the dome's automation suite.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither — there is no monitoring code for this specific ratio. The dome's W019 weekly test asks a related but distinct question (does poleward drift dominate lateral) and the dome itself marked it REFINED, conceding that lateral drift dominates 5.5×. WIN-043 and W019 are about the same physical asymmetry but the dome reports them as inconsistent ratios (2.26× CONFIRMED vs. 5.5× REFINED).
data_sources_used: NOAA NGDC NP.xy pole-position time series (1590-2025; same dataset as WIN-006/007/036)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 2.26× value lacks methodological specification: no time window, no velocity method (instantaneous vs. average), no coordinate system (geographic vs. geomagnetic), no unit basis (km/yr vs. degrees/yr). Our quantitative verification (this cycle, see quantitative_verification field) shows the dome's value IS approximately reproducible — but only under a specific choice of (a) km/yr units and (b) a recent ~2018-2024 window. Without specification, it is recoverable but the dome could disclaim a future ratio change as 'wrong window' if the post-2025 trajectory diverges.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No discrepancies. Our review does not over-claim what the dome's monitoring does.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Per C1/C2 chain (no monitor.py available in current raw-text), Domain #11 only validates longitude > 130°E threshold. No automation computes velocity-component ratios. The 2.26× value is unmonitored homepage text. Tag unchanged from C2.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard geophysics fully explains the directional asymmetry: Livermore et al. 2020 (Nature Geoscience, doi:10.1038/s41561-020-0570-9) attributes the predominantly longitudinal drift to competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes at the core-mantle boundary. The dome renames the cause ('aetheric vortex threshold crossing') but provides no alternative numerical derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: NOAA NP.xy data is publicly indexed and predates the dome model by decades — the underlying observations are available back to 1590. The C3 rewrite added an explicit argument tying this to Part 3.1b's legitimate-novelty carve-out: indexed public datasets fail the 'not plausibly known to author at write-time' standard. Our forward-looking sibling claim PRED-R004 (≥2.0× through 2028) is recycled from this same post-hoc retrodiction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives the 2.26× ratio from dome parameters. The dome's H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) field equation is axially symmetric — it depends only on radial distance from the dome center and structurally cannot produce longitude-dependent quantities. The Axial Symmetry Paradox section explicitly names WIN-043 as one of the longitude-dependent claims that this radially-symmetric geometry cannot underwrite.

holes_found.description: MISSING ARGUMENT: W019 status flipped PENDING → FALSIFIED since the C2 review (2026-04-14, ISS-951). The dome's own weekly directional test for the NMP — claim was 'poleward dominates lateral' — was REFINED by the author after observed lateral drift was 5.5× larger than poleward. WIN-043's current text does not mention this. The argument that 'the dome's own monitoring contradicts the static 2.26× number' is now much stronger than C2 noted, because the dome has self-reported a 5.5× lateral:poleward ratio in W019's REFINED notes — a value that doesn't reconcile with WIN-043's claimed 2.26× without invoking different epochs or methodologies (which the dome doesn't specify). This is a self-contradiction within the dome's own data: WIN-043 (CONFIRMED, ratio=2.26×) and W019 (REFINED, dome-reported ratio=5.5×) disagree by a factor of 2.4, both for the same physical asymmetry.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_extra (or extend detail_evidence): 'The dome's own weekly test W019 was registered claiming poleward NMP drift would dominate over lateral. The author marked W019 REFINED on the basis that observed lateral drift is 5.5× larger than poleward — a value 2.4× higher than WIN-043's claimed 2.26× ratio. The dome's own monitoring suite therefore reports two different ratios for the same directional asymmetry (2.26× as a CONFIRMED past claim, 5.5× in current REFINED notes), without methodological reconciliation. The directional asymmetry is real — but the dome cannot produce a stable predicted value for it.'

holes_found.description: CARRYOVER FROM C2 (UNADDRESSED): The C2 stronger_arguments recommended cross-referencing the Axial Symmetry Paradox section (Part 4 in current numbering, was Part 4.5 pre-V6). That recommendation was never integrated into WIN-043's detail_extra. Currently WIN-043 stands on its own without connecting to the systemic structural critique that names it explicitly. A one-sentence cross-reference would link the per-WIN argument to the structural one and reinforce both.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_extra: 'See Section 4 (Axial Symmetry Paradox): the dome's H(r) field equation depends only on radial distance, so by construction it cannot generate longitude-dependent quantities of any magnitude — 2.26× or otherwise. WIN-043 is one of 12+ WINs cataloged there as longitude-dependent claims from a radially symmetric model.'

holes_found.description: Subtle wording in finding: 'standard core-mantle boundary dynamics explains the same drift anisotropy without a dome' is correct but could be marginally tighter. Standard geophysics doesn't 'predict' the 2.26× ratio either (no model independently outputs that number) — it explains why the asymmetry exists (flux-lobe competition) and the observed ratio falls out naturally as a description. The current 'explains' wording is fine; this is noted only because the C2 review flagged the same point. No change required.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed. Current 'explains' framing is precise.

stronger_arguments.description: The W019 falsification narrative (currently absent from WIN-043's text) gives us a stronger version of the C2 weak-form argument 'dome can't confirm its own weekly test.' The dome has now adjudicated that test as REFINED with a specific contradicting ratio (5.5× lateral vs. 2.26× claimed), and this is documented in the verdict_history of our predictions.json. Adding this turns WIN-043 from 'descriptive retrodiction' into 'descriptive retrodiction the dome itself contradicts' — substantially stronger.
sources: data/predictions.json — W019 entry, verdict_history showing standard_physics → falsified flip 2026-04-14 (ISS-951)
Author REFINED status with notes 'lateral drift dominates, not poleward'

stronger_arguments.description: PRED-R004 (NMP drift ratio ≥2.0× through 2028) gives us a forward-looking falsification opportunity. The dome has implicitly committed to ≥2.0× holding through 2028 (recycled from WIN-043). Quantitative verification this cycle confirms the 2020-2025 km/yr ratio is ~2.3× (close to claimed 2.26×) — but the 2024-2025 single-year ratio is 1.56× and decelerating. If NMP continues decelerating per the post-2019 trend (Chulliat et al. 2020), PRED-R004 could fall below 2.0× before 2028, falsifying the recycled claim. Worth flagging as a watchable forward test in our review (probably in Part 5 Kill Shots or wherever PRED-R004 is tracked).
sources: NOAA NP.xy 2024-2025 single-year velocity components (this review's Lens C verification)

kernel_of_truth.description: The directional asymmetry of NMP drift is real and quantitatively significant: NOAA NP.xy data confirms longitudinal velocity has dominated latitudinal velocity for the entire post-1990 trajectory. Computed in km/yr units over 1990-2025, the ratio is 2.16× — within 5% of the dome's claimed 2.26×. The dome correctly identifies a feature most laypeople wouldn't notice (data/predictions.json:W019 was registered exactly to track this asymmetry).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three independent failures persist. (1) Post-hoc retrodiction: the NOAA NP.xy dataset (https://ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy) predates the dome model by decades and is publicly indexed — it fails the Part 3.1b 'not plausibly known at write-time' carve-out, as our new detail_verdict_text now correctly argues. (2) No dome derivation: the dome's H(r) field is axially symmetric (data/wins.json:WIN-043 detail_evidence acknowledges this implicitly; sections.json Axial Symmetry Paradox section names it explicitly) and structurally cannot produce longitude-dependent quantities. (3) Self-inconsistency: the dome's own W019 weekly test (data/predictions.json:W019.notes) reports a 5.5× lateral:poleward ratio in REFINED notes — 2.4× higher than WIN-043's 2.26× — for the same physical asymmetry, with no reconciliation.

advocate_mode.best_defense: Speaking as the dome defender: your review's central case against WIN-043 is that the 2.26× ratio is 'unverifiable / unspecified.' But your own quantitative work this cycle shows it IS verifiable — over a 1990-2025 km/yr velocity window the ratio is 2.16×, within 5% of our claim. We can specify the methodology (km/yr velocity averaged over the modern epoch where the directional asymmetry is most pronounced) and the value reproduces. Your post-hoc objection cuts both ways: yes, the data exists publicly, but its directional asymmetry is non-obvious — random readers don't extract this ratio without prompting. Calling it post-hoc treats every measurement of nature as illegitimate, which is too strict a standard. Standard geophysics may explain WHY the asymmetry exists (flux lobes), but it doesn't independently derive 2.26×. We do — by pointing at it. That's a kind of explanatory work, even if not an a priori prediction.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The defense rated 3/5 because the 'verifiability' point is partially correct (we showed it ourselves this cycle). To preempt: (a) the C3 detail_verdict_text already addresses this via Part 3.1b — extend it slightly to acknowledge 'the value is reproducible from public data, but reproducibility-from-public-data is the definition of post-hoc retrodiction, not a defense against it'; (b) add the W019 self-contradiction (Hole #1 above) to dissolve the 'we point at it, that's explanatory work' angle: pointing at it inconsistently (2.26× CONFIRMED but 5.5× REFINED for the same phenomenon) is not explanatory.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-006 (NMP exponential acceleration), WIN-007 (NP-1990 phase transition), WIN-036 (NMP position prediction), and WIN-043 (directional ratio) all derive from the same NOAA NP.xy dataset. Our reviews across all four consistently classify them as post-hoc retrodictions from public data with no dome-geometry derivation. The arguments reinforce rather than contradict. WIN-007 and WIN-043 specifically both make the structural-symmetry point (axial symmetry vs. longitude-dependent claim). No internal contradiction found.

claims_checked: Dome claim: longitudinal/latitudinal NMP drift ratio = 2.26×
Our review claim (current text): the value is 'epoch-dependent'
C2 review's working hypothesis (not in current text): 'naïve full-trajectory yields ratio far larger than 2.26×'
quantitative_verification.computations: Fetched NOAA NGDC NP.xy (https://ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy, 437 lines, 1590-2025). Computed |Δlon|·cos(lat_avg)·R vs. |Δlat|·R (km components) over multiple windows. Results: 1900-2025 → 1.69×; 1990-2025 → 2.16×; 2000-2025 → 2.70×; 2010-2025 → 9.43×; 2020-2025 → 2.34× (annual velocity); 2024-2025 → 1.56× (most recent). DEGREE-based computation (the C2 hypothesis) yields 1990-2025 → 11.13× and 2010-2025 → 117× — much larger than 2.26×, but this is a units artifact (longitude degrees become physically tiny near the geographic pole).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The dome's 2.26× IS approximately reproducible (NOAA km/yr 1990-2025: 2.16×; 2020-2025: 2.34×). The C2 review's hypothesis that 'naïve computation yields a much larger ratio' was a degree/km units confusion — true in degrees, false in km. The CURRENT review text avoids this trap by saying 'epoch-dependent' generically, which is correct (the ratio swings from 1.69× to 9.43× depending on window). Our argument should NOT claim the 2.26× is unrecoverable; it should claim it's a post-hoc retrodiction that the dome cannot independently derive. Our text already does this in C3 — no correction needed.

deeper_analysis:
C3 review of WIN-043 (data drift triggered: detail_verdict_length +249%, detail_evidence_length +68%). The C3 rewrite (which drove the drift) added an explicit post-hoc-test failure paragraph to detail_verdict_text, cross-referencing Part 3.1b's legitimate-novelty carve-out. Verified: (a) the cross-reference target #posthoc-standard exists in sections.json:part3.html; (b) the argument structure is sound — NOAA NP.xy IS publicly indexed and IS the kind of dataset Part 3.1b targets; (c) the consistency claim 'no monitor.py entry exists for this value' is consistent with our code_analysis_tags.monitoring='none' (carried unchanged from C2). The post-hoc paragraph is a strong addition.

Key gap: W019 status changed PENDING (C2) → FALSIFIED (current). The dome's author marked W019 REFINED with notes 'lateral drift is 5.5× larger than poleward.' WIN-043's text does not reference this. C2 had a weak-form version of this argument ('dome can't confirm its own weekly test'); now we have a strong-form version ('dome itself adjudicated a contradicting 5.5× ratio for the same asymmetry, vs. WIN-043's 2.26× CONFIRMED'). This is Hole #1 (major).

Quantitative verification (Lens C, run this cycle): I fetched NOAA NP.xy and computed velocity-component ratios over multiple windows. The dome's 2.26× is approximately reproducible in km/yr units — 1990-2025 yields 2.16×; 2020-2025 yields 2.34×. Our current text saying 'epoch-dependent' is correct (the ratio swings from 1.69× to 9.43× by epoch) but the C2 hypothesis that 'naïve calculation yields far larger ratio' was a degree/km units confusion. Helpful that current text doesn't make this specific claim.

Advocate Mode (Lens A): the dome defender's strongest move is to argue 'the value is verifiable, just unspecified-but-reasonable methodology.' Rated 3/5 because partially correct (we verified it). Preempted by adding the W019 self-contradiction (Hole #1) and by the existing post-hoc paragraph correctly framing reproducibility-from-public-data as the definition of post-hoc, not a defense against it.

Cross-WIN consistency (Lens B): WIN-043 reinforces rather than contradicts WIN-006, WIN-007, WIN-036 (all NOAA NP.xy-based, all post-hoc retrodictions, all citing axial symmetry as a structural problem). No internal contradictions in our reviews.

Verdict: Std Model Explains holds. Recommended action: minor_edit to add the W019 self-contradiction and the Axial Symmetry Paradox cross-reference (carryover from C2 unaddressed). Confidence 0.93.
